Computer-Assisted Reporting Newsletter, 1990–2007

Browse Topics Search

When TRI is not enough, try state hazardous waste manifests

Adam Berliant Founder of EPIC October 1992
Data & DatabasesGovernment & PoliticsBusiness & Economy

Hazardous waste manifests provide detailed tracking of toxic materials that can supplement federal TRI data, with about half of U.S. states offering computerized access to these records for investigative journalism purposes. Reporters can use manifest data to identify illegal dumping, verify environmental compliance, and enhance local accountability in environmental stories.

The article discusses using state hazardous waste manifests to track hazardous materials. TRI provides generalized data, while manifests give detailed tracking 'from cradle to grave,' such as who transports and where materials go. Reporters can use this data to track hazardous waste, adding detail to TRI records. About half of U.S. states are computerized with hazardous waste data. The article suggests contacting state agencies for data and points out the variability in data access and costs.

EPIC offers resources for journalists looking to use these data. This approach can identify illegal activities and align local figures with federal data, enhancing environmental reporting.
